children.name: Iguana
children.description: True iguanas.

children.name: Iguana iguana
children.description: Most commonly traded reptile globally; strictly herbivorous; arboreal; drops into water when threatened; adult males display large dewlaps and dorsal crests; invasive in Florida.

children.name: Iguana delicatissima
children.description: Critically endangered; hybridising with introduced Green Iguana across Caribbean islands.

children.name: Amblyrhynchus
children.description: The marine iguana.

children.name: Amblyrhynchus cristatus
children.description: The only truly marine lizard; forages on subtidal algae; can shrink its skeleton during food scarcity by reabsorbing bone; expels excess salt in explosive nasal sneezes; Darwin considered them imps of darkness; IUCN Vulnerable.

children.name: Brachylophus
children.description: Fiji iguanas.

children.name: Brachylophus bulabula
children.description: Bright green with pale blue bands; described as a distinct species in 2008; inhabits moist forests on Viti Levu, Ovalau and Kadavu; primarily arboreal and herbivorous; threatened by habitat loss and invasive predators.

children.name: Brachylophus fasciatus
children.description: Bright green with white or pale blue transverse bands; males develop more prominent bands; native to the Lau Islands, Fiji, and introduced to Tonga; arboreal in humid forests; feeds on leaves, fruit and flowers; IUCN Endangered.

children.name: Brachylophus gau
children.description: Described in 2017 and endemic to Gau Island, Fiji; previously confused with B. bulabula and B. fasciatus; distinguished by genetic and morphological analysis; restricted to a single island, making it highly vulnerable to extinction.

children.name: Brachylophus vitiensis
children.description: Bright green with white chevrons and a tall dorsal crest; restricted to dry forests of the Yasawa, Mamanuca and Yadua Taba islands; the global population is about 13,000, with 12,000 on the 70-hectare Yadua Taba; IUCN Critically Endangered.

children.name: Cachryx
children.description: Yucatán spiny-tailed iguanas.

children.name: Cachryx alfredschmidti
children.description: Gray-green with black markings and reddish accents on the neck and abdomen; found in lowland deciduous forest and seasonally flooded scrub in southern Campeche, Mexico and adjacent Guatemala; arboreal, hiding in hollow branches; named after German herpetoculturist Alfred Schmidt.

children.name: Cachryx defensor
children.description: Small iguana with a short, strikingly spiny tail; inhabits dry forests and limestone areas of the Yucatán Peninsula; secretive and often overlooked, known by the Mayan name Choop; commonly found basking on ancient Mayan stone walls.

children.name: Conolophus
children.description: Galápagos land iguanas.

children.name: Conolophus subcristatus
children.description: Large herbivore of Galápagos interior; mutualistic grooming by Darwin's finches; examined by Darwin on Isabela Island; IUCN Vulnerable.

children.name: Conolophus marthae
children.description: Striking pink body with bold black vertical bands; restricted to the summit of Wolf Volcano on northern Isabela Island; described as a new species in 2009; the pink coloration may result from lack of skin pigmentation combined with red blood cell anomalies; only ~200 individuals remain; IUCN Critically Endangered.

children.name: Conolophus pallidus
children.description: Pale cream-yellow with a long tapered snout and pronounced dorsal spines; endemic to Santa Fe Island in the Galápagos; primarily herbivorous, with prickly-pear cactus making up 80% of its diet; shares a mutualistic tick-removal relationship with Darwin's finches.

children.name: Ctenosaura
children.description: Spiny-tailed iguanas.

children.name: Ctenosaura similis
children.description: The fastest lizard on Earth, recorded at 34.9 km/h; largely herbivorous as adults; invasive in Florida.

children.name: Ctenosaura acanthura
children.description: Large spiny-tailed iguana found along the Gulf coast of Mexico from Tamaulipas to Veracruz; prefers dry lowland forests and thorn scrub; primarily herbivorous but may take insects as juveniles; tail rings of sharp spines give the genus its name.

children.name: Ctenosaura bakeri
children.description: Known locally as the swamper; uniquely adapted to mangrove swamps on Utila Island, Honduras; dark gray-brown with a short tail; critically endangered from habitat destruction for tourism development; females lay eggs in termite mounds.

children.name: Ctenosaura brachylopha
children.description: Yellowish body coloration distinguishes it from the closely related C. pectinata; ranges from Sinaloa to Nayarit on the Pacific coast of Mexico, including Isla Clarión; resurrected from synonymy in 2019 based on mitochondrial and microsatellite genetic data.

children.name: Ctenosaura clarki
children.description: Endemic to the Balsas Basin in Michoacán, Mexico; inhabits dry tropical deciduous forest; small to medium-sized with a long spiny tail; threatened by habitat conversion for agriculture in the Balsas depression.

children.name: Ctenosaura conspicuosa
children.description: Endemic to San Esteban Island in the Gulf of California; closely related to C. hemilopha but restricted to a single island; exhibits insular adaptations including a darker coloration; shares its island home with the piebald chuckwalla Sauromalus varius.

children.name: Ctenosaura flavidorsalis
children.description: Named for the distinctive yellow dorsal stripe; found in Honduras, El Salvador and Guatemala; inhabits dry tropical forests and thorn scrub; diurnal and arboreal; one of the least-studied Ctenosaura species.

children.name: Ctenosaura hemilopha
children.description: The only spiny-tailed iguana native to Baja California Sur; short dorsal crest; gray to brown with darker crossbands; inhabits rocky arroyos and dry canyons; feeds on leaves, flowers and fruits; females guard their nests aggressively.

children.name: Ctenosaura macrolopha
children.description: Restricted to Sonora, Mexico; distinguished by a longer dorsal crest than other Ctenosaura; inhabits dry tropical forest and thorn scrub; gray-brown with faint banding; remains poorly studied.

children.name: Ctenosaura melanosterna
children.description: Named for the distinctive black patch on the chest; endemic to the Río Aguán Valley and Cayos Cochinos in Honduras; inhabits dry forests and coastal areas; threatened by habitat loss and illegal hunting; IUCN Endangered.

children.name: Ctenosaura nolascensis
children.description: Endemic to San Pedro Nolasco Island in the Gulf of California; similar in appearance to C. hemilopha but isolated on a single small island; only a few hundred meters of coastline make up its entire habitat.

children.name: Ctenosaura oaxacana
children.description: Small iguana restricted to the Pacific versant of the Isthmus of Tehuantepec in Oaxaca, Mexico; very little is known about its natural history; inhabits dry forests and thorn scrub; IUCN Endangered.

children.name: Ctenosaura oedirhina
children.description: Endemic to Roatán Island, Honduras; distinguished by a swollen snout; gray-brown with darker crossbands; inhabits rocky coastal areas and dry forests; severely threatened by tourism development; IUCN Endangered.

children.name: Ctenosaura palearis
children.description: Endemic to the Motagua Valley in Guatemala; one of the most threatened Ctenosaura species; inhabits dry thorn forests and cactus scrub; males have a distinctive pale throat region; CITES Appendix II; IUCN Endangered.

children.name: Ctenosaura pectinata
children.description: One of the larger Ctenosaura species, reaching 1.3 m; native to western Mexico and introduced to southern Texas and Florida; social and group-living, unlike most other spiny-tailed iguanas; excellent climber that prefers rocky habitats with crevices.

children.name: Ctenosaura quinquecarinata
children.description: Named for the five longitudinal keels of spiny scales on the tail; ranges from southern Mexico through Nicaragua to northwestern Costa Rica; hatchlings are bright green, fading to brown with age; males develop blue, yellow and black tones.

children.name: Cyclura
children.description: Caribbean rock iguanas.

children.name: Cyclura cornuta
children.description: Formidable bony protuberances on snout; critically endangered on Haiti and Dominican Republic; slow reproductive rate makes recovery difficult.

children.name: Cyclura carinata
children.description: The smallest Cyclura species, reaching only 30 cm SVL; endemic to the Turks and Caicos Islands and Booby Cay in the Bahamas; keeled scales give the species its name; severely impacted by introduced predators and habitat loss; IUCN Critically Endangered.

children.name: Cyclura collei
children.description: Once thought extinct; rediscovered in 1990 in the Hellshire Hills of Jamaica; greenish-gray with dark olive bands; the largest native land vertebrate on Jamaica; a head-starting program has boosted the wild population to several hundred; IUCN Critically Endangered.

children.name: Cyclura cychlura
children.description: Large and heavy-bodied, reaching 1.4 m; found on Andros, Exuma and Allen Cays in the Bahamas; males display striking yellow, orange or red scales on the head; three subspecies recognized, each restricted to different island groups; IUCN Vulnerable.

children.name: Cyclura lewisi
children.description: Powder-blue body that intensifies during the breeding season; one of the longest-living lizards, with a lifespan exceeding 60 years; an ongoing conservation success story, recovered from 10-25 wild animals in 2002 to over 1,000 today; IUCN Endangered.

children.name: Cyclura nubila
children.description: Large, gray-brown iguana with dark bands and a clouded pattern; found throughout Cuba and introduced to Puerto Rico; females guard burrows aggressively; two subspecies: the nominate on Cuba and C. n. caymanensis on Little Cayman and Cayman Brac; IUCN Vulnerable.

children.name: Cyclura onchiopsis
children.description: Now extinct; formerly endemic to Navassa Island in the Caribbean, between Hispaniola and Jamaica; had prominent temporal swellings and likely shared the rhinoceros-like horn of its closest relatives; last recorded in the 1960s; habitat destruction from guano mining and introduced predators caused its demise.

children.name: Cyclura pinguis
children.description: Stocky, robust body with a blunt snout; once widespread across the Puerto Rico Bank, now restricted to Anegada in the British Virgin Islands; fewer than 200 adults remain in the wild; the largest surviving native land animal on Anegada; IUCN Critically Endangered.

children.name: Cyclura ricordii
children.description: Grayish-brown with a pronounced crest and bold dark bands; endemic to Hispaniola, where it coexists sympatrically with the larger C. cornuta; the only Cyclura species known to share its range with a congener; threatened by charcoal production and grazing; IUCN Endangered.

children.name: Cyclura rileyi
children.description: Medium-sized Cyclura restricted to San Salvador, Acklins and Sandy Cay in the Bahamas; three subspecies occur on different cays, each isolated by water; coloration varies from gray to dark brown; populations number only a few hundred per island; IUCN Endangered.

children.name: Cyclura stejnegeri
children.description: Endemic to Mona Island, Puerto Rico; formerly considered a subspecies of C. cornuta but elevated based on diagnostic scale characters and genetic isolation; larger horns than the rhinoceros iguana; dark gray with a massive head; threatened by feral pigs and goats.

children.name: Dipsosaurus
children.description: Heat-tolerant desert iguanas.

children.name: Dipsosaurus dorsalis
children.description: One of the most heat-tolerant lizards; active at temperatures lethal to other lizards; herbivorous on flowers and leaves.

children.name: Sauromalus
children.description: Chuckwallas.

children.name: Sauromalus ater
children.description: Large, robust lizard with a flattened body and loose folds of skin; found from the southwestern USA to Baja California and Sonora, Mexico; inflates its body to wedge itself immovably in rock crevices when threatened; strictly herbivorous, feeding on leaves, fruit and flowers of desert shrubs.

children.name: Sauromalus hispidus
children.description: Large black chuckwalla with enlarged tuberculate and spiny scales on the head and neck; endemic to Ángel de la Guarda Island and ten smaller islands in the Gulf of California; can reach 60 cm and 2 kg; one of the larger chuckwalla species.

children.name: Sauromalus klauberi
children.description: Endemic to Santa Catalina Island in the Gulf of California; distinguished by a spotted dorsal pattern; medium-sized with a relatively short tail; named after American herpetologist Laurence M. Klauber; restricted to a single island, making it vulnerable to extinction.

children.name: Sauromalus slevini
children.description: Found on Islas Carmen, Coronados and Monserrate in the Gulf of California; named after Joseph R. Slevin; smaller than other insular chuckwallas; inhabits rocky volcanic slopes; IUCN Vulnerable due to its restricted island distribution.

children.name: Sauromalus varius
children.description: The largest chuckwalla, reaching up to 60 cm and 1.4 kg; strikingly patterned with irregular patches of dark and light pigmentation, giving a piebald appearance; endemic to San Esteban Island in the Gulf of California; primarily herbivorous, feeding on desert shrubs and cacti.
